spark.observation: /qor-substantiate Step 7 appends entries to docs/META_LEDGER.md using sequential numbering (Entry #1, #2, etc.) which is fundamentally single-writer. In a multi-worker federation session, Entries #16, #17, and #18 each appeared twice from different concurrent sessions working different issues (Canonical Event Schema #376 / Accountable Live #335 / Migration Pipeline #332). Chain-hash linkage stayed intact (each worker read latest at append time) but sequential ordering was corrupted.
spark.initial_question: How should META_LEDGER preserve sequential-ordering invariants under concurrent federation append without breaking the cryptographic chain or requiring centralized coordination?
spark.why_now: Federation is no longer hypothetical -- FED-0001 through FED-0026 dispatches have been observed in production. Every concurrent seal cycle has a non-trivial chance of producing duplicate-numbered entries until this is closed. Each duplicate degrades audit-trail readability and confuses downstream tooling that assumes sequential entries.

affected_actors: Federation operators (multiple workers running concurrent SDLC cycles against the same ledger)
Downstream auditors / compliance reviewers who rely on sequential entry numbers to navigate the ledger
Tools that parse META_LEDGER (currently assume sequential entries: seal_entry_check, ledger_hash.verify, badge_currency counter)
Qor-logic maintainers (every duplicate entry is a future SG entry + downstream-tool patch)
problem_frame.failure_mode: Concurrent writers race on entry-number allocation. Worker A reads 'latest is #15', appends '#16: A's work'. Worker B (started before A's commit) also reads 'latest is #15', appends '#16: B's work'. Both reads were correct at the moment they were taken; the entry-number space has no atomic-allocation mechanism. The cryptographic chain survives (chain_hash uses previous_hash which is content-addressable) but human-readable structure breaks.
problem_frame.cost_of_failure: Audit trail readability degrades; seal_entry_check and ledger_hash.verify don't detect duplicate numbering today (they validate per-entry chain math but not entry-number uniqueness); badge_currency overcounts entries; manual reconciliation post-incident is non-trivial; risk compounds as federation scales.

transformation_statement: META_LEDGER moves from sequential-numbering single-writer assumption that silently produces duplicate entries under concurrent federation append to content-addressable entry IDs + topological ordering by previous_hash chain that tolerates concurrent append while preserving cryptographic chain integrity without breaking existing audit-tool assumptions, requiring federation workers to coordinate via locks, or producing seals that downstream consumers can't trust.

non_goals: NOT eliminating the human-readable 'Entry #N' label entirely -- operators and auditors are accustomed to it; the V1 fix can add content-addressable IDs alongside, not replace.
NOT building a distributed-consensus protocol (Raft / Paxos / Tendermint) for ledger writes -- overkill for the actual scale.
NOT introducing a centralized append broker -- federation should remain leaderless.
limitations: V1 may require a one-time ledger reconciliation pass to renumber the existing duplicate entries (#16a/b, #17a/b, #18a/b) into a consistent ordering. This is a forward-only operation (immutable Merkle chain after the renumber commit).
The chain-integrity guarantee remains previous_hash-anchored; entry numbers become advisory display, not load-bearing ordering.
exclusions: No changes to chain_hash / content_hash algorithms -- only the numbering / display scheme.
No changes to existing seal-ceremony Steps 7.4-7.8 (SSDF tags, badge currency, gate-chain-completeness) beyond what's needed to consume the new numbering.
forbidden_interpretations: This is NOT permission to drop entry numbers entirely. Auditor-facing readability matters. The V1 design must preserve a deterministic, human-readable numbering even when allocated post-hoc via topological sort.
This is NOT a green light to break backward compatibility for past ledger entries. Existing Entries #1-#206 stay numbered as-is.

options_matrix.name: Content-addressable entry IDs + post-hoc sequential renumber
options_matrix.summary: Each entry gets a primary ID SHA256(timestamp + phase + content_hash)[:12]. Sequential 'Entry #N' label is allocated by topological sort of previous_hash chain at seal time (or at a periodic reconciliation step). seal_entry_check extended to detect previous_hash uniqueness violations (catches concurrent races).
options_matrix.selection_reason: Matches the issue's proposed fix. Preserves chain integrity (already-correct under concurrency). Catches duplicates structurally. Backward-compatible: past entries keep their numbers; new entries get content-addressable ID + sequential label.

options_matrix.name: Session-scoped ledger segments
options_matrix.summary: Each session writes to docs/META_LEDGER-{session_id}.md; segments merge into the canonical META_LEDGER.md at periodic reconciliation. Eliminates concurrent append entirely.
options_matrix.rejection_reason: Larger surface: requires merge logic, conflict resolution between segments, fresh tooling for ledger viewers. Filer raised it as alternative; the simpler content-addressable approach achieves the same goal with less mechanism.

options_matrix.name: File-lock the append step
options_matrix.summary: /qor-substantiate Step 7 acquires an OS-level advisory lock on META_LEDGER.md before append.
options_matrix.rejection_reason: Introduces a single-writer bottleneck that defeats federation parallelism. Doesn't work across machines / network filesystems. Workers might deadlock.

options_matrix.name: No fix; document the limitation
options_matrix.summary: Add a doctrine entry catalogueing concurrent-append as known-unsafe. Operators serialize federation manually.
options_matrix.rejection_reason: Honest but unsatisfying -- the federation pattern is documented as first-class; making operators serialize manually contradicts that.

evidence_required: Tests proving content-addressable ID derivation is deterministic and collision-resistant (synthetic-collision fixtures)
Tests proving topological renumber produces the same sequential labels regardless of write-order (commutative under previous_hash chain)
Tests proving seal_entry_check detects duplicate previous_hash claims (the new concurrency-race signature)
Tests proving past Entries #1-#206 survive the change unchanged (backward-compat regression suite)
Tests proving badge_currency counter correctly counts entries under the new scheme
Synthetic federation-race fixture: two workers simultaneously appending; assert exactly one survives or both get distinct numbers via topological sort
Doctrine entry SG-ConcurrentLedgerRace-A catalogueing the Entry #16/17/18 duplicate-numbering incident
escalation_triggers: If implementation finds existing ledger_hash.verify semantics are incompatible with topological renumber, escalate to /qor-research before continuing -- verify-then-build is critical given chain blast radius.
If the synthetic-collision fixture finds any hash-collision for SHA256[:12] truncation at realistic scale, escalate to /qor-plan to extend ID length or use full-hash.
If the renumber commit produces any entry whose number/content combo doesn't match a past auditor's expectation, escalate to /qor-remediate before publishing.

failure_remediation_plan.failure_class: Topological-sort renumber produces non-deterministic results across re-runs
failure_remediation_plan.detection_signal: Test fixture: run the renumber twice; assert byte-identical output
failure_remediation_plan.containment_action: Tighten tiebreaker (e.g., add (session_id, ts) as secondary sort key); ABORT seal if non-deterministic

failure_remediation_plan.failure_class: Content-addressable ID collision (two different entries hash to same 12-char prefix)
failure_remediation_plan.detection_signal: Test fixture with synthetic-collision dataset; hash-distribution analysis on real ledger
failure_remediation_plan.containment_action: Extend ID length to 16 or 20 chars; or use full 64-char hash with display-truncation

failure_remediation_plan.failure_class: previous_hash uniqueness check catches a real federation race after deployment
failure_remediation_plan.detection_signal: New concurrent-ledger-race event in Process Shadow Genome; seal abort emits diagnostic
failure_remediation_plan.containment_action: Operator decides whether to merge the two competing entries via reconciliation pass (forward-only) or revert one branch

failure_remediation_plan.failure_class: Backward-compat regression: an existing tool (badge_currency, audit viewer) breaks under new numbering
failure_remediation_plan.detection_signal: CI regression suite fails on a pinned-version snapshot of past ledger entries
failure_remediation_plan.containment_action: Revert to Option 4 (document limitation) temporarily; root-cause the dependency on numbering semantics

failure_remediation_plan.detection_signal: Git diff on META_LEDGER.md shows lines changed in already-sealed Entries #1-#206
failure_remediation_plan.containment_action: ABORT publish; doctrine entry forbids retroactive renumbering

readiness.rationale: Problem is concrete (Entry #16/17/18 duplicates have actual identifiers), fix is well-defined (Option 1 content-addressable + topological sort), evidence requirements are enumerable. The L3 risk grade means /qor-plan should include high_risk_target: true and impact_assessment per EU AI Act dialogue. No /qor-research needed -- the proposed solution is technically straightforward; the work is implementing it carefully against the high-risk surface.
